chapters.summary: Genealogy of Jesus; virgin birth foretold and fulfilled
chapters.manuscripts_note: P1 (~200–250 AD) contains Matthew 1:1–9, 12, 14–20 — the earliest known papyrus fragment of Matthew

chapters.summary: Visit of the Magi; flight to Egypt; massacre of the innocents; return to Nazareth
Ministry of John the Baptist; baptism of Jesus; voice from heaven
Temptation of Jesus in the wilderness; beginning of Galilean ministry; calling of the first disciples
The Sermon on the Mount begins: Beatitudes, salt and light, fulfillment of the Law, six antitheses
Sermon on the Mount: almsgiving, prayer (Lord's Prayer), fasting, treasures in heaven, anxiety
chapters.manuscripts_note: The doxology ending of the Lord's Prayer (v. 13b) is absent from earliest manuscripts

chapters.summary: Sermon on the Mount concludes: judging others, ask–seek–knock, narrow gate, false prophets, two builders
Healing the leper, centurion's servant, Peter's mother-in-law; stilling the storm; Gadarene demoniacs
Healing the paralytic; calling of Matthew; raising Jairus's daughter; healing the blind and mute
Commissioning of the twelve apostles; instructions for mission
John the Baptist's question; Jesus's testimony about John; woes on unrepentant cities; the yoke of Christ
Lord of the Sabbath; healing the withered hand; Beelzebul controversy; the unforgivable sin; sign of Jonah
Parables: Sower, Wheat and Tares, Mustard Seed, Leaven, Hidden Treasure, Pearl, Dragnet
Death of John the Baptist; feeding the 5,000; Jesus walks on water
Traditions of the elders; Syrophoenician woman; feeding the 4,000
Peter's confession at Caesarea Philippi; Jesus announces the church; first passion prediction
Transfiguration; healing the epileptic boy; second passion prediction; temple tax
chapters.manuscripts_note: Verse 21 ('Howbeit this kind goeth not out but by prayer and fasting') is absent from the oldest Greek manuscripts

chapters.summary: The greatest in the kingdom; offenses; the lost sheep; church discipline; forgiveness; unmerciful servant
chapters.manuscripts_note: Verse 11 ('For the Son of man is come to save that which was lost') is absent from the oldest Greek manuscripts

chapters.summary: Divorce; blessing the children; the rich young ruler; rewards for disciples
Parable of the workers; third passion prediction; request of James and John; healing Bartimaeus
Triumphal entry; cleansing the Temple; cursing the fig tree; authority questioned; parable of the two sons; wicked tenants
Parable of the wedding feast; taxes to Caesar; resurrection question; greatest commandment; David's son
Seven woes against the scribes and Pharisees; lament over Jerusalem
Olivet Discourse: destruction of the Temple, signs of the end, coming of the Son of Man, no one knows the hour
chapters.manuscripts_note: Verse 36 contains a significant variant — 'nor the Son' appears in the best Greek manuscripts but was omitted by many later copyists

chapters.summary: Parables of the ten virgins, talents; the sheep and the goats
Anointing at Bethany; Last Supper and institution of the Eucharist; Gethsemane; betrayal and arrest; Peter's denial; Jesus before Caiaphas
Trial before Pilate; death of Judas; crucifixion; death and burial of Jesus
chapters.manuscripts_note: A small minority of manuscripts add an Old Testament quotation to verse 35 about casting lots

chapters.summary: The resurrection; the Great Commission

1:23.text: Behold, a virgin shall be with child, and shall bring forth a son, and they shall call his name Emmanuel, which being interpreted is, God with us.

greek_words.english: and shall bring forth a son
and they shall call his name

earliest_manuscript.name: P1 (Oxyrhynchus Papyrus 2)
earliest_manuscript.description: The earliest surviving papyrus fragment of the Gospel of Matthew, containing portions of Matthew 1:1–9, 12, 14–20. Written in Greek on papyrus, discovered at Oxyrhynchus, Egypt.

earliest_church_father_citation.author: Ignatius of Antioch
earliest_church_father_citation.quote: For our God, Jesus Christ, was conceived by Mary in accord with God's plan: of the seed of David, it is true, but also of the Holy Spirit.

variants.description: The word translated 'virgin' (παρθένος, parthenos) is sometimes debated. Matthew quotes Isaiah 7:14 from the Septuagint (Greek OT), which uses παρθένος. The underlying Hebrew word is עַלְמָה (almah), which some scholars translate as 'young woman' rather than 'virgin.' However, all Greek manuscripts of Matthew 1:23 uniformly use παρθένος, which specifically means 'virgin' in Greek.
variants.manuscripts_supporting: All ~1,750 Greek manuscripts use παρθένος. There is no manuscript tradition that replaces this word with νεᾶνις ('young woman') or any other term.
variants.scholarly_consensus: Textually, the verse is completely stable. The debate is hermeneutical (about Isaiah's original intent), not textual. Matthew's inspired quotation of the LXX definitively uses 'virgin.'
variants.doctrinal_impact: The virgin birth is one of the most foundational doctrines of Christianity. The manuscript evidence is unanimous on what the text says. Whether Jesus was born of a virgin is a theological claim Matthew asserts without textual ambiguity.

1:23.plain_english_summary: Matthew 1:23 is textually rock-solid. Every Greek manuscript agrees on the word 'virgin' (παρθένος). The verse quotes Isaiah 7:14 from the Greek Old Testament and has been central to Christian confession since the earliest church fathers. The debate about this verse is entirely about Old Testament interpretation — not what Matthew actually wrote. All manuscripts agree on the text.

5:17.text: Think not that I am come to destroy the law, or the prophets: I am not come to destroy, but to fulfil.

greek_words.english: I am not come to destroy

earliest_manuscript.name: Codex Sinaiticus (א)
earliest_manuscript.description: One of the two oldest and most complete Greek New Testament manuscripts. Contains the entire New Testament including this verse.

earliest_church_father_citation.author: Irenaeus of Lyon
earliest_church_father_citation.quote: He did not abrogate but fulfilled [the law].

variants.description: A handful of late manuscripts and some Syriac versions substitute 'the Prophets' with 'the writings' or omit 'or the prophets' entirely. This appears to be a scribal simplification.
variants.manuscripts_supporting: Only a small number of late Greek manuscripts (post-9th century) show any variation. The vast majority — including all early papyri and the great uncials — read 'the law, or the prophets.'
variants.scholarly_consensus: The full reading 'the law, or the prophets' is original. There is no serious textual dispute here.
variants.doctrinal_impact: None. The verse is textually stable and its meaning — that Jesus came to fulfill, not abolish, the Mosaic covenant — is universally attested.

5:17.plain_english_summary: Matthew 5:17 is textually among the most stable verses in the New Testament. Every major manuscript tradition records Jesus's declaration that He came to fulfill the Law and the Prophets. The verse was quoted extensively by church fathers from the late 2nd century onward, long before the great codices were copied. There is no meaningful textual dispute about what Matthew wrote here.

6:9.text: After this manner therefore pray ye: Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name.

greek_words.english: After this manner therefore pray ye

earliest_manuscript.name: Codex Sinaiticus (א)
earliest_manuscript.description: One of the oldest complete New Testament manuscripts. The Lord's Prayer text in Matthew 6 is intact.

earliest_church_father_citation.author: Tertullian
earliest_church_father_citation.quote: Our Father which art in heaven...

6:9.plain_english_summary: The opening of the Lord's Prayer is textually secure across all manuscript traditions. It appears in virtually identical form in over 1,745 Greek manuscripts and all early versions. The Didache (a Christian text from ~100 AD) also preserves the Lord's Prayer in a form very close to Matthew's, providing one of the earliest external attestations.

6:13.text: And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil: For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, for ever. Amen.

greek_words.greek: ἀλλὰ ῥῦσαι ἡμᾶς ἀπὸ τοῦ πονηροῦ

earliest_manuscript.name: Codex Sinaiticus (א)
earliest_manuscript.description: Codex Sinaiticus notably OMITS the doxology ('For thine is the kingdom...') — a significant early witness that the doxology was a later liturgical addition.

earliest_church_father_citation.author: Didache (Teaching of the Twelve Apostles)
earliest_church_father_citation.quote: ...but deliver us from the evil one; for thine is the power and the glory forever.

variants.description: The doxology — 'For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, for ever. Amen.' — is present in the majority of Greek manuscripts but absent from the earliest and best witnesses. Codex Sinaiticus, Codex Vaticanus, and many early Latin manuscripts all end the prayer at 'deliver us from evil.' The doxology appears to be a liturgical addition that entered the manuscript tradition from early Christian worship practice.
variants.manuscripts_supporting: The doxology appears in the Byzantine majority text (post-7th century) and is found in around 1,600+ later Greek manuscripts. It is absent from Sinaiticus (א), Vaticanus (B), the Vulgate, and many early church fathers including Origen, Tertullian, and Cyprian.
variants.scholarly_consensus: Nearly all modern critical editions (NA28, UBS5) omit the doxology from the main text and place it in brackets or footnotes. Most scholars believe the doxology was adapted from 1 Chronicles 29:11 and added for use in corporate worship by the late 1st–early 2nd century.
variants.doctrinal_impact: Moderate. The doxology is beautiful and doctrinally sound — but its liturgical origin means most modern translations either omit it or footnote it. The KJV and NKJV include it; the ESV, NIV, and NASB do not (or bracket it).

6:13.plain_english_summary: Matthew 6:13 presents the most well-known textual variant in the Lord's Prayer. The prayer itself ('lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil') is textually rock-solid. The doxology that follows it — 'For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, forever. Amen' — is present in most later manuscripts but missing from the oldest Greek codices and many early church writers. It was likely added in early Christian worship (the Didache shows a shorter version of it as early as ~100 AD) and gradually entered the manuscript tradition. It is doctrinally accurate but not original to Matthew's text.

16:18.text: And I say also unto thee, That thou art Peter, and upon this rock I will build my church; and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it.

greek_words.english: And I say also unto thee

earliest_manuscript.name: Codex Sinaiticus (א)
earliest_manuscript.description: Codex Sinaiticus preserves this verse intact. The Greek wordplay on Πέτρος (Peter, 'rock') and πέτρα ('bedrock') is clear in the original.

earliest_church_father_citation.author: Origen
earliest_church_father_citation.quote: Peter, upon whom the Church of Christ is built...

variants.description: There are no significant textual variants in Matthew 16:18. The entire debate over this verse is theological and hermeneutical — specifically whether 'this rock' refers to Peter himself, to Peter's confession of faith, or to Christ. This is a matter of interpretation, not of what the manuscripts say.
variants.manuscripts_supporting: All Greek manuscripts read identically. The Greek wordplay (Πέτρος/πέτρα) is universally preserved.
variants.scholarly_consensus: Textually, this verse is among the most stable in Matthew. The word 'church' (ἐκκλησίαν) is notable as one of only two times Jesus uses this word in the Gospels (the other is 18:17). No manuscript omits or alters it.
variants.doctrinal_impact: High theological significance — this verse is central to debates between Roman Catholicism, Eastern Orthodoxy, and Protestantism about papal authority. But all sides debate the same text; no one disputes what Matthew wrote.

16:18.plain_english_summary: Matthew 16:18 is textually one of the most stable verses in the Gospels — not a single Greek manuscript alters, omits, or questions any word in it. The enormous theological debate this verse generates (Catholic vs. Protestant interpretations of 'this rock') is entirely about interpretation, not about the manuscript evidence. Every tradition agrees on what Matthew wrote. This is a case where the text itself is crystal clear; only its meaning is disputed.

17:21.text: Howbeit this kind goeth not out but by prayer and fasting.
17:21.bracket_note: Matthew 17:21 is absent from Codex Sinaiticus (א), Codex Vaticanus (B), and multiple early versional witnesses. It appears to be a scribal harmonization from Mark 9:29, inserted by later copyists who were familiar with Mark's parallel account. All major modern critical editions (NA28, UBS5) either omit this verse entirely or place it in brackets. The KJV and NKJV include it following the Byzantine text tradition.

greek_words.greek: εἰ μὴ ἐν προσευχῇ καὶ νηστείᾳ

earliest_manuscript.name: Codex Bezae (D)
earliest_manuscript.description: Codex Bezae is among the early manuscripts that do include Matthew 17:21, though the oldest and most reliable witnesses (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) omit it.

earliest_church_father_citation.author: Origen
earliest_church_father_citation.quote: Some kinds of demons are driven out by prayer and fasting.

variants.description: Matthew 17:21 is entirely absent from the oldest and most reliable Greek manuscripts. Following verse 20 (the mustard seed teaching on faith), the oldest texts jump directly to verse 22. The inserted verse reads exactly like Mark 9:29 and appears to be a scribal harmonization — a copyist who knew Mark's account added the verse to bring Matthew into alignment.
variants.manuscripts_supporting: The verse appears in the Byzantine majority text (later manuscripts) and is in approximately 65% of surviving Greek manuscripts. However, it is absent from P45, Sinaiticus (א), Vaticanus (B), the Coptic tradition, and key Old Latin witnesses.
variants.scholarly_consensus: Almost all textual scholars regard Matthew 17:21 as a later interpolation from Mark 9:29. It is omitted in the critical text (NA28/UBS5). Many modern translations (ESV, NIV, NASB) either omit it entirely or include it only in a footnote.
variants.doctrinal_impact: Low doctrinal impact — the concept of prayer and fasting as spiritual disciplines is well-attested throughout the New Testament (see Mark 9:29, Luke 2:37, Acts 13:3, 1 Cor. 7:5). Omitting this verse removes nothing that isn't said elsewhere.

17:21.plain_english_summary: Matthew 17:21 is one of the clearest cases of scribal harmonization in the Gospels. The two oldest and most reliable Greek manuscripts — Sinaiticus and Vaticanus — completely skip from verse 20 to verse 22. The verse appears only in later manuscripts and is essentially identical to Mark 9:29. The evidence strongly suggests a copyist inserted it to align Matthew with Mark. Most modern Bible translations either omit this verse entirely or relegate it to a footnote, explaining that it is not found in the earliest manuscripts.

18:11.text: For the Son of man is come to save that which was lost.
18:11.bracket_note: Matthew 18:11 is absent from the oldest and most reliable Greek manuscripts, including Codex Sinaiticus (א), Codex Vaticanus (B), Codex Ephraemi Rescriptus (C), and several Old Latin witnesses. It appears to be a scribal insertion harmonized from Luke 19:10 ('For the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost'). All major modern critical editions omit it from the main text.

greek_words.greek: ἦλθεν γὰρ ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου
greek_words.english: For the Son of man is come
to save that which was lost

earliest_manuscript.name: Codex Alexandrinus (A)
earliest_manuscript.description: Codex Alexandrinus is among the manuscripts that include Matthew 18:11. The older and more reliable witnesses (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) omit it.

earliest_church_father_citation.author: Chrysostom
earliest_church_father_citation.quote: He came to seek and to save the lost.

variants.description: Matthew 18:11 does not appear in the oldest Greek manuscripts. It is virtually identical to Luke 19:10 ('For the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost') and is likely a harmonization — a copyist inserted the Lukan saying into Matthew's account of the lost sheep parable to make the parallel explicit.
variants.manuscripts_supporting: Present in the Byzantine majority text and roughly 60% of later Greek manuscripts. Absent from Sinaiticus (א), Vaticanus (B), Ephraemi (C), the Sinaitic Syriac, and many Old Latin manuscripts.
variants.scholarly_consensus: The verse is considered secondary by virtually all textual critics. NA28 and UBS5 omit it. The ESV, NIV, and NASB omit it from the main text (with footnotes). The KJV and NKJV include it following the Byzantine tradition.
variants.doctrinal_impact: None. Luke 19:10 preserves the identical statement in a clearly original context. The doctrine of Jesus coming to seek and save the lost is unambiguously taught — this verse's omission from Matthew removes nothing unique.

18:11.plain_english_summary: Matthew 18:11 is, like verse 17:21, a verse that the oldest manuscripts do not include. The two most important early Greek codices (Sinaiticus and Vaticanus) both omit it, and it is word-for-word parallel to Luke 19:10 — a strong sign that a scribe inserted it from memory. The verse is beautiful and theologically true, but it is almost certainly not what Matthew originally wrote at this location. Modern critical translations consistently omit it or footnote it.

24:36.text: But of that day and hour knoweth no man, no, not the angels of heaven, but my Father only.

earliest_manuscript.name: Codex Sinaiticus (א)
earliest_manuscript.description: Codex Sinaiticus includes the phrase 'nor the Son' (οὐδὲ ὁ υἱός) in Matthew 24:36 — the same phrase found in Mark 13:32. This is significant because many later Byzantine manuscripts omit it in Matthew.

earliest_church_father_citation.author: Origen
earliest_church_father_citation.quote: Of that day or hour no one knows, not even the Son of Man, but the Father only.

variants.description: The key textual variant in Matthew 24:36 is the presence or absence of the phrase 'nor the Son' (οὐδὲ ὁ υἱός). Many later Greek manuscripts — the Byzantine majority text — omit this phrase. But the oldest and best manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, and the parallel in Mark 13:32) include it. The phrase appears to have been deliberately omitted by some later scribes who found it theologically difficult to affirm that the Son did not know the hour.
variants.manuscripts_supporting: The phrase 'nor the Son' is in Sinaiticus (א), Vaticanus (B), Ephraemi (C), Codex Bezae (D), the Old Latin, Syriac Peshitta, and Origen (3rd century). It is absent from the Byzantine majority text and many later manuscripts. Mark 13:32, the parallel passage, includes 'nor the Son' in virtually all manuscripts with no debate.
variants.scholarly_consensus: Critical scholars almost universally accept 'nor the Son' as original to Matthew 24:36. Its omission in later manuscripts is a classic case of theological editing (scribes uncomfortable with the Son's apparent limitation of knowledge). NA28 and UBS5 include 'nor the Son' in brackets. Most modern translations include it.
variants.doctrinal_impact: Significant theological discussion — early theologians used this verse in Christological debates about the divine and human natures of Christ. Orthodox Christianity explains it through the doctrine of the two natures: in His human nature, the Son voluntarily limited His knowledge.

24:36.plain_english_summary: Matthew 24:36 contains one of the most theologically interesting textual variants in the Gospels. The phrase 'nor the Son' — affirming that even Jesus did not know the exact day and hour of the second coming — is present in the oldest and best manuscripts but was removed by later copyists who were uncomfortable with what it implies about Christ's knowledge. The parallel verse in Mark 13:32 has 'nor the Son' in virtually every manuscript without dispute. Most textual scholars agree the phrase belongs in Matthew as well. Modern translations generally include it.

27:35.text: And they crucified him, and parted his garments, casting lots: that it might be fulfilled which was spoken by the prophet, They parted my garments among them, and upon my vesture did they cast lots.

greek_words.greek: ἵνα πληρωθῇ τὸ ῥηθὲν ὑπὸ τοῦ προφήτου
greek_words.english: that it might be fulfilled which was spoken by the prophet
They parted my garments among them

greek_words.greek: καὶ ἐπὶ τὸν ἱματισμόν μου ἔβαλον κλῆρον
greek_words.english: and upon my vesture did they cast lots

earliest_manuscript.name: Codex Sinaiticus (א)
earliest_manuscript.description: Codex Sinaiticus preserves the short reading of Matthew 27:35 — 'And they crucified him, and parted his garments, casting lots' — without the Old Testament fulfillment quotation.

earliest_church_father_citation.author: Origen
earliest_church_father_citation.quote: They divided his garments and cast lots for his clothing.

variants.description: Many important manuscripts of Matthew 27:35 contain only the brief account: 'And they crucified him and divided his garments, casting lots.' The longer reading — which adds the fulfillment quotation from Psalm 22:18 — is found in a number of later manuscripts. This appears to be a scribal harmonization from John 19:24, where the fulfillment quote does appear in the original text (and likely in all manuscripts). Copyists familiar with John's explicit reference inserted it into Matthew's shorter account.
variants.manuscripts_supporting: The short reading (without the OT quotation) is in Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, and many early versions. The longer reading with the quotation appears in various later Greek manuscripts, the Byzantine tradition, and some Latin witnesses. John 19:24 includes the quotation in virtually all manuscripts — it is original there.
variants.scholarly_consensus: Critical editions (NA28, UBS5) follow the shorter reading. The OT quotation in Matthew 27:35 is considered a later addition harmonized from John 19:24. The KJV follows the longer Byzantine reading.
variants.doctrinal_impact: Low. The fulfillment of Psalm 22:18 is explicitly stated in John 19:24 in all manuscripts. Omitting the quotation from Matthew loses nothing doctrinal — the event itself and its scriptural fulfillment are still attested.

27:35.plain_english_summary: Matthew 27:35 exists in two forms. The older, shorter version simply records that Jesus was crucified and that soldiers divided His garments by casting lots — a direct fulfillment of Psalm 22:18. Later manuscripts add the explicit Old Testament quotation, apparently because scribes copied it in from John 19:24, where the same event is narrated with the explicit quote. Most modern translations follow the shorter reading in Matthew while retaining the full quotation in John, where it originally belongs.

28:19.text: Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost:

earliest_manuscript.name: Codex Sinaiticus (א)
earliest_manuscript.description: Codex Sinaiticus preserves Matthew 28:19 intact, including the full Trinitarian formula. Every surviving Greek manuscript of this verse reads identically.

earliest_church_father_citation.author: Justin Martyr
earliest_church_father_citation.quote: ...in the name of God, the Father and Lord of the universe, and of our Saviour Jesus Christ, and of the Holy Spirit, they then receive the washing with water.

variants.description: Eusebius of Caesarea (263–339 AD), in his early writings, frequently quotes the Great Commission as 'in my name' (ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου) rather than the Trinitarian formula. This has led some scholars to claim the Trinitarian formula was a later addition. However, there is not a single Greek manuscript — not one out of 1,750 — that supports this shorter reading. Eusebius himself later quotes the full Trinitarian formula, suggesting his earlier quotations were paraphrases rather than citations of a different text.
variants.manuscripts_supporting: Zero Greek manuscripts support the 'in my name only' reading. All 1,750+ surviving Greek manuscripts, all Latin manuscripts, all Syriac manuscripts, all Coptic manuscripts, and all other versional evidence agree on the full Trinitarian formula.
variants.scholarly_consensus: The Trinitarian formula is universally acknowledged as original. The Eusebius argument is an argument from patristic quotation style, not from manuscript evidence. No critical scholar proposes emending this text. The Trinitarian formula is as well-attested as any verse in the New Testament.
variants.doctrinal_impact: This verse is foundational to the Christian doctrine of the Trinity and to the practice of Trinitarian baptism. Its manuscript attestation is complete — no tradition, language, or manuscript family omits or alters the Trinitarian formula.

28:19.plain_english_summary: Matthew 28:19 is among the best-attested verses in the entire New Testament. Every single Greek manuscript — all 1,750+ — contains the exact same Trinitarian formula: 'in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit.' The occasional claim that Eusebius of Caesarea had a different text ('in my name') is based on his loose quotation style, not on any manuscript. The Great Commission has been copied, cited, and used in baptismal rites since the earliest Christian centuries. Its textual foundation is unshakeable.

28:20.text: Teaching them to observe all things whatsoever I have commanded you: and, lo, I am with you alway, even unto the end of the world. Amen.

greek_words.english: even unto the end of the world

earliest_manuscript.name: Codex Sinaiticus (א)
earliest_manuscript.description: The final verse of Matthew's Gospel is preserved in full in Codex Sinaiticus and in every other manuscript of Matthew. The promise 'I am with you always' is among the most cherished words in all of Scripture — and its textual foundation is perfectly secure.

earliest_church_father_citation.author: Eusebius of Caesarea
earliest_church_father_citation.quote: I am with you all the days, until the end of the age.

28:20.plain_english_summary: Matthew 28:20 is the final verse of the Gospel — and it is textually flawless. Every surviving manuscript of Matthew agrees on every word. Christ's promise 'I am with you always, even to the end of the age' was cherished by the early church, copied faithfully across every language and tradition, and cited by church fathers from the 3rd century onward. This verse has no textual variants of any significance. It is exactly as Matthew wrote it.

27:46.text: And about the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice, saying, Eli, Eli, lama sabachthani? that is to say, My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?

greek_words.english: Jesus cried with a loud voice

earliest_manuscript.name: Codex Sinaiticus (א)
earliest_manuscript.description: Codex Sinaiticus preserves this verse intact. The Aramaic cry — quoting Psalm 22:1 — is preserved with slight spelling variations across manuscripts but is universally attested.

earliest_church_father_citation.author: Justin Martyr
earliest_church_father_citation.quote: My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?

variants.description: The Aramaic words in this verse (ηλι ηλι λεμα σαβαχθανι) are spelled with slight variations across manuscripts — reflecting differences in how Greek scribes transcribed Aramaic sounds. Some manuscripts have Ηλι (Eli) while others have Ελωι (Eloi, matching Mark 15:34). This is a minor orthographic variant with no impact on meaning.
variants.manuscripts_supporting: The 'Ηλι Ηλι' reading follows the Hebrew of Psalm 22:1; the 'Ελωι Ελωι' reading follows the Aramaic form in Mark. Both readings appear in different manuscript streams of Matthew. The bystanders' confusion about Elijah (v. 47) makes more sense with 'Eli' than 'Eloi.'
variants.scholarly_consensus: Both readings are early and attested in important manuscripts. Most critical editions prefer 'Eli Eli' for Matthew (distinct from Mark's 'Eloi Eloi'), reflecting the Hebrew/Aramaic distinction. The difference is purely phonetic — the meaning is identical.
variants.doctrinal_impact: None. Jesus's cry from the cross — quoting Psalm 22:1 — is the most theologically profound moment in the passion narrative. All manuscripts, in all traditions, agree that He cried out 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'

27:46.plain_english_summary: Matthew 27:46 records Jesus's desolation cry from the cross — a direct quotation of Psalm 22:1 in Aramaic. The verse is textually secure across all major manuscript traditions. The only minor variation is in the spelling of the Aramaic words (slight differences between Hebrew and Aramaic forms of 'My God'), which is expected when Greek-speaking scribes transcribed a Semitic language. Every tradition — Greek, Latin, Syriac, Coptic, Armenian — agrees on what Jesus cried. This is one of the most raw, emotionally charged moments in all of Scripture, and it is preserved exactly.

5:18.text: For verily I say unto you, Till heaven and earth pass, one jot or one tittle shall in no wise pass from the law, till all be fulfilled.

greek_words.english: For verily I say unto you

greek_words.greek: ἕως ἂν παρέλθῃ ὁ οὐρανὸς καὶ ἡ γῆ
οὐ μὴ παρέλθῃ ἀπὸ τοῦ νόμου
greek_words.english: shall in no wise pass from the law

earliest_manuscript.name: Codex Sinaiticus (א)
earliest_manuscript.description: Codex Sinaiticus preserves this verse in full. The famous 'jot and tittle' language — referring to the smallest marks in the Hebrew alphabet — is universally attested.

earliest_church_father_citation.author: Irenaeus of Lyon
earliest_church_father_citation.quote: One jot or one tittle shall not pass from the law.

5:18.plain_english_summary: Matthew 5:18 is textually perfect across all manuscript traditions. Jesus's dramatic declaration — that not even the smallest letter (iota, the Greek equivalent of the Hebrew yod, the 'jot') or the smallest stroke (the 'tittle,' a tiny decorative mark on Hebrew letters) will pass from the Law until all is fulfilled — is attested in every Greek manuscript without any meaningful variation. This verse, ironically, is one of the most stable texts in the entire Sermon on the Mount.
